canonical_or_religious_significance: In Jain tradition, most important Jain Tirtha where twenty of twenty-four Tirthankaras including Parshvanatha attained Moksha; holiest pilgrimage site for both Digambara and Śvētāmbara sects
historical_significance: Located in Giridih district of Jharkhand. Highest mountain peak in Jharkhand at 1365 meters. Hill named after Parshvanatha 23rd Jain Tirthankara who in Jain belief attained salvation here. For each Tirthankara there is shrine (gumti or Tonk) on hill. Jaina temple believed to have been constructed by Magadha King Bimbisara. Cunningham noted stone structures in village describing them as remnant of Buddhist stupa datable to 2nd century BCE. Beautiful Jain temple on mountain peak known as Svarna Bhadra Koot made of marble. Another marble Jain temple on hill known as Jal Mandir. Ancient idol of Lord Parasnath located in valley at Palganj believed to be 2500 years old. Large Digambara Jain temple depicting Nandishwar Dweep at base of hill. Nichli temple built by Calcutta merchant in 18th century noteworthy for architecture featuring arched gateways and carvings of Tirthankaras on temple wall.

Located near Bhubaneswar in Odisha. Important historical and archaeological sites reflecting Jain monastic life. Caves date back to 2nd century BCE built during reign of King Kharavela of Mahameghavahana dynasty. Excavated in 1st century BCE to 1st century CE. Udayagiri has 18 caves while Khandagiri has 15 caves with 4 of each having significant features. Rani Gumpha largest cave in Udayagiri built in 2 floors in U shape. Most important cave 14 Hathi Gumpha has 17 line inscription discussing achievements of King Kharavela since crowned at age 24 including public works and conquests. Re-discovered and brought under government control in 1825 by A. Stirling. Caves honey-combed rock-cut structures commissioned by King Kharavela. Protected by Archaeological Survey of India.

Śvetāmbara Jain temple located at Badridas Temple Street Gouribari in Maniktala Kolkata. Major tourist attraction of Kolkata. Temple construction in 1867 paid for by philanthropist Rai Badridas Bahadoor Mookim. Consecration performed by Jain acharya named Kalyansuri Maharaja. Temple complex consists of four temples. Main shrine dedicated to Shitalanatha, 10th Tirthankara. Temple also known as Parshwanath Temple as entire complex dedicated to both Parshvanath who was 23rd Jain Tirthankara and Shitalanatha. One of most famous and important Jain temples in Kolkata. Devout Jains foreigners visitors and pilgrims of other faiths from distant parts of India visit temple precincts year round. Image of Lord Shitalanatha seated in sanctum sanctorum and his diamond-studded forehead major attraction for visitors. Temple remains open for public from 6 AM to 11 AM and 3 PM to 7 PM. Temple exquisite beautiful featuring mirrors and coloured mirrors everywhere. Complex has four temples. Arboretum with multiple effigy and couple of tanks flooded with multiple type of fishes. Never extinguishing lamp located in main temple.

canonical_or_religious_significance: In Jain tradition, birthplace of Lord Munisuvrata 20th Tirthankara
historical_significance: Famous pilgrimage centre for Jains located in Bihar. Also known as Panch Pahadi. Famous as Jain Teerth from ancient times. Place was capital of King Jarasandha contemporary of Lord Krishna and Lord Neminatha. Contains 12 temples (10 on mountain and 2 at base) spread across 5 mountains with total 4189 steps. Excavation revealed huge ancient Jain temple with 24 rooms. Fifth mountain Vaibhargiri has six Jain temples including Shri Parshwanath Bhagwan Shri Mahavir Swami Shri Munisuvrat Swami Shri Basupujya Bhagwan Shri Dhannashali Bhadra and Shri Gautam Swami temple. Contains their charan padukas and idols. In Jain tradition, on mountain Gautam Swami along with his 11 gandhars attained nirvana. First mountain Vipulachal has beautiful temple of Bhagwan Munisuvrat Swami containing idol of Munisuvrat Bhagwan and charan padukas of Adinath Swami and Mahavir Swami. On way Aimuttamuni temple. Temple was renovated in 2007.

Located near Gulzarbagh Railway Station in Patna. In Jain tradition, site where famous Jain teacher Sthulabhadra (297-198 BCE) spent his last days. Part of Jain circuit of Bihar holds special significance for people of Jainism. Temple built in 1729 CE to commemorate Sthulabhadra. Temple houses statue of religious guru Sudarshan Swami and commemorative shrine for Jain saint Sthulabhadra. Built on brick mound one of oldest Jain temples in Patna. Contains inscription dating to 1792 CE giving details of construction. Inscription confirms that Patliputra existed here. Ancient Jain temple complex situated near Gulzarbagh railway station. Temple dedicated to famous Jain saint Sundarshan Swami. Ancient Jain temple maintained by Patna group of Jain Shwetambar Temple Committee. Complex has two Jain temples. One located on mound dedicated to Sthulbhadra of Maurya time muni. Other temple located closely dedicated to Sudarshan.

Digambar Jain temple in Bhubaneswar Odisha located atop Khandagiri hill. Hill honeycombed by series of rock-cut Jaina caves commissioned by King Kharavela in 1st century BCE. Rock-cut caves protected by Archaeological Survey of India. According to local traditions built by King Kharavela of Mahameghavahana dynasty in 1st century BCE. Alexander Cunningham estimated temple structure built during Maratha Empire based on architectural design. Present temple built in first quarter of 19th century. Architecturally in Pidha style with Vimana and Jaga mohan following Kalinga construction. Temple built of Makardha stone in Ashlar way on triratha plan and raised tri-anga bada. Important pilgrimage site for Digambara sect of Jainism. Temple complex consists of three temples. First temple houses colossal black stone idol of Parshvanatha in white marble hall. Main shrine houses white marble idol of Mahavira along with large number of Jain idols. Third shrine houses 5 images of Jain tirthankaras. Maintained by Bengal Bihar and Odisha Digambara Jaina Tirthankara Committee.
